name: Collins v. Virginia
summary: A police officer, following up on a stolen motorcycle lead, walked up the driveway of a private home and looked under a tarp parked in an attached carport to confirm the vehicle's identity, without obtaining a warrant. The Court held that the automobile exception to the warrant requirement does not permit officers to enter the curtilage of a home to search a vehicle.
holding: The automobile exception does not extend to vehicles located within the curtilage of a home. The curtilage receives the same Fourth Amendment protection as the home itself, and no categorical exception displaces that protection merely because the item to be searched is a vehicle.

legal_definitions.definition: The area immediately surrounding and associated with a home that shares the Fourth Amendment protections afforded to the home itself; typically includes attached garages, driveways, and enclosed yards immediately adjacent to the dwelling.
legal_definitions.context: The Court reaffirmed that the curtilage is constitutionally equivalent to the home for Fourth Amendment purposes, rejecting any automobile exception that would override this protection.

relevance_to_audit: Limits the automobile exception at curtilage boundary; relevant to analysis of vehicle-focused surveillance programs that may extend searches into residential property.

key_quotes.text: The automobile exception does not permit an officer without a warrant to enter a home or its curtilage in order to search a vehicle therein.
key_quotes.context: Holding that the automobile exception yields to curtilage protection when the two doctrines conflict.

procedural_posture: Virginia Supreme Court reversed suppression order; reversed
subsequent_treatment: Applied to drone surveillance of curtilage and ALPR capture of vehicles parked in residential driveways
